Most movements are comprised of sequences.
From the complex routines that gymnasts perform to intricate dance numbers to simply reaching for an object, our movements are made up of sequences of movements that are learned through practice.
The cerebellum is the brain region that controls the movement of the eye, and it is thought to be involved in learning and executing these sequences.
However, it is not clear how this process works.
Previous studies have shown that the brain can learn sequences of single-component movements, such as eye movements, but not sequences of multi-joint movements.
This suggests that the cerebellar system is involved in the learning and execution of sequences, but it is unclear how it does so.
To address this question, Halverson et al.
studied how the brain learns sequences of movement components in rabbits.
The experiments used a technique called eyelid conditioning, which involves stimulating the brain cells that control eye movements by stimulating them with mossy fibers.
The mice were then asked to perform a sequence of eye movements in response to an auditory cue.
The rabbits were then instructed to repeat the sequence of movements until they reached the end of the sequence.
Halverson and Bastian then recorded the brain activity of the rabbits as they performed the movements.

Imagine a gymnastics competition in which participants take turns to cartwheel and somersault across the floor.
The routines on display comprise sequences of precisely timed movements learned through practice.
This is also true for many of the actions we perform every day, such as reaching for a cup of coffee.
A region of the brain called the cerebellum helps us learn sequences of movements.
But how does it do this?
To find out, Khilkevich et al.
came up with a new version of an old experiment.
Rabbits were first trained to blink their eye in response to a specific external cue.
This type of learning, called associative learning, has been shown before in the cerebellum.
But Khilkevich et al.
wondered whether the cerebellum could also use internal feedback signals from the eyeblink as a cue to learn the next movement?
If so, this might explain how the cerebellum can chain movements together in a sequence.
As predicted, Khilkevich et al.
found that rabbits could learn to blink their eye in response to an initial signal, and then blink again in response to the first blink.
Control experiments confirmed that the second eyeblink was coupled to the first, and not to the original cue.

Mammalian cells use a protein called mTOR to control the growth and metabolism of their tissues and organs.
This protein is part of a group of proteins called the mTOR complex, which is also known as mTORC1 or mTOR2.
In humans, mTOR is a complex of two proteins that are responsible for controlling the growth of the cell.
However, it is not clear how mTOR interacts with other proteins to form the complex, and how it interacts with rapamycin, a drug that prevents mTOR from working properly.
Now, Saxton et al.
have used a technique called cryo-electron microscopy to study the structure of mTOR in yeast cells grown in the laboratory.
The experiments show that mTOR forms a complex with two other proteins called Raptor and SIN1, which are important components of the complex.
The other protein Raptor binds to a region of the protein called the FRB domain that is unique to mTOR.
This region is also the site where the other mTOR proteins bind to each other, and it is thought that this binding site is the same for all mTOR complexes.

To grow and multiply, a living cell must take a variety of factors into account, such as its own energy levels and the availability of nutrients.
A protein called mTOR sits at the core of a signaling pathway that integrates these and other sources information.
Problems with the mTOR pathway contribute to several diseases including diabetes and cancer.
The mTOR protein occurs in two distinct protein complexes, called mTORC1 and mTORC2.
These complexes contain a mix of other proteins – known as accessory proteins.
They also sense different cues and act upon distinct targets in the cell.
Recent research reported the structure of mTORC1, which provided clues about how this complex works.
Yet, much less was known about the mTORC2 complex.
Stuttfeld, Aylett et al.
have now used a technique called cryo-electron microscopy to reveal the three-dimensional architecture of the human version of mTORC2.
Comparing the new mTORC2 structure to the existing one for mTORC1 showed that they have many features in common but important differences too.
The overall shape of both complexes is similar and each complex contains two copies of mTOR arranged in a similar way.

Our perception of number and space are tightly intertwined.
This is due to the fact that the brain’s neural circuits that process space and numbers both localize to the same area of the brain called the parietal cortex.
However, the two areas can be distinguished based on their respective receptive field properties: while neurons in area LIP have spatially restricted receptive fields, each covering only a portion of the visual world, those in area VIP have a wider receptive field that covers the entire visual world.
It has been suggested that this co-localization is the result of ‘cortical recycling’, where numerical processing takes over circuits originally processing space.
But to what extent do such recycled circuits retain their original functionality?
Does numerosity replace space, or does it augment it?
To investigate this question, Dehaene et al.
used a new phenomenon in which visual motion direction, a spatial dimension ubiquitous in dorsal stream areas, adapts numerosity, giving rise to a repulsive aftereffect: motion to the left adapts small numbers, leading to an overestimation of numerosity.
Motion to the right adapts large numbers, resulting in underestimation.
This suggests that numerosity and space processing both co-exist in areas of the
ref:
Our sense of number is thought to have emerged from the circuits of cortical neurons in the brain that originally represent space, a process known as 'cortical recycling'.
Accordingly, our perception of space and number are tightly intertwined: for example, people think about numbers on a mental number line, where smaller numbers are mapped to the left, and larger numbers are mapped to the right.
Also, damage to a brain region called the parietal cortex disrupts both space and number processing.
If number processing recycles the neurons that encode space, which form does this appropriation take?
Recycling could preserve the original behavior of the neurons ( processing space ), thus enriching the neurons’ functional repertoire with a new capacity ( processing number ).
Alternatively, the newly developed role could replace the original one, such that space and number cohabitate the same brain area but use separate neurons.
To disentangle these hypotheses, Schwiedrzik et al.
used a technique called 'perceptual adaptation'.
Here, continuously showing someone a particular feature eventually exhausts the neurons that respond to that feature.
Neurons that respond to the opposite feature are however less exhausted and dominate perception.
Consequently, people perceive the opposite of what they are adapted to.

Stem cells are a type of stem cell that can divide into a wide variety of specialized cells, including neurons, glial cells, and epithelial cells that line the surface of the blood, tissues and organs of the body.
These specialized cells must be able to sense and respond to nutrients in their environment, and they must also produce the proteins and other molecules needed for their specialized cells to work properly.
A protein called Musashi-1 is essential for stem cells to differentiate into specialized cells.
However, it is not clear how this process works.
In the central nervous system, for example, the stem cells called oligodendrocytes are able to divide into neurons and glial and neural progenitor cells, but not in mature neurons.
This is because they lack a protein called MSI1, which binds to RNA molecules and prevents them from being translated into the proteins needed for the cells to divide.
This prevents the cells from producing the proteins required for the cell to divide properly.
To find out more about this process, Sakakibara et al.
used a technique called EMSA to measure the activity of a protein that binds to a molecule called CCCR005.
This protein binds to the N-terminal region of RNA molecules
ref:
When an embryo is developing, stem cells must divide and develop into many specialized types of cells.
However, if cell division doesn't stop, or if it restarts later in life, it can cause tumors to form.
Musashi-1 is a protein that binds to molecules of RNA and helps to promote cell growth during development: mice that lack this protein have serious brain defects and die shortly after birth.
Musashi-1 is usually turned off in adult cells that are not dividing.
Sometimes, however, it remains active and contributes to the growth of cancers in the brain and the gut.
Reducing Musashi-1 levels in colon tumors slows their growth and causes the cancer cells to die.
To find a compound that would switch off Musashi-1, Clingman et al.
screened more than 30, 000 compounds and identified four inhibitors.
One of these was oleic acid, a fatty acid that is found in olive oil and other animal and plant oils.
Oleic acid interacts with Musashi-1 in a way that changes the shape of the protein.
These changes mean that Musashi-1 is no longer able to regulate the genes that control cell proliferation.

also found that Musashi-1 promotes the activity of a

The nervous system is made up of thousands of nerve cells that communicate with each other by sending electrical signals to and from nerve cells called synapses.
These nerve cells are connected to each other via a series of connections known as synapses, and the electrical signals travel along these connections in a process called synaptic transmission.
The electrical signals are carried by chemical molecules called synaptic vesicles, which are released from the synapses when the synapse is stimulated.
These molecules are stored in a pool called the readily releasable pool ( RRP ), which can be exhausted during high frequency stimulation ( HFS ), but can be replenished during low-frequency stimulation ( or HFS for short ).
The amount of SV in the RRP depends on the amount of calcium ions that are present in the pool, which is known as the Ca2+, and this is regulated by a protein called syt 7.
Syt 7 is a protein that is found in all mammals, but it is not clear what role it plays in the nervous system.
Previous studies have suggested that it is important for synapses to be able to replenish the pool of SV.

ref:
Neurons communicate with one another at junctions called synapses.
The arrival of an electrical signal called an action potential at the first neuron triggers the release of chemicals called neurotransmitters into the synapse.
These chemicals then diffuse across the gap between the neurons and bind to receptors on the second cell.
The neurotransmitter molecules are stored in the first cell in packages known as vesicles, which release their contents by fusing with the cell membrane.
Following a fusion event, neurons must replenish their vesicle stocks to ensure that they are ready for the arrival of the next action potential.
This replenishment process is known to involve a calcium-dependent pathway and a calcium-independent pathway.
A protein called calmodulin, that binds calcium ions, has an important role in the first of these pathways.
Now, Liu et al.
have shown that another protein, synaptotagmin 7, also has a key role in the replenishment of synaptic vesicles, possibly as a sensor for calcium ions.
Moreover, Liu et al.
found that synaptotagmin 7 and calmodulin bind to each other to form a complex, which suggests that the calcium-dependent replenishment pathway is regulated by this complex.

Mood and anxiety disorders are the most common psychiatric conditions and are exacerbated by stress.
Treatments for these disorders are currently limited to drugs that work by blocking the activity of a protein called cyclooxygenase-2, which is found in the brain.
However, it is not clear whether these drugs are effective in treating these disorders, or whether they could be used as a treatment for other psychiatric conditions, such as major depression or anxiety disorders.
To investigate this question, Hermanson et al.
studied the effects of two different types of COX-2 inhibitors on mice that had been exposed to stressful situations.
The experiments showed that the COX inhibitors reduced anxiety-like behaviors in mice that were exposed to stress, and that the SSCIs did the same in the absence of these stress-induced behaviors.
In addition, both COX and SSCI inhibitors reduced the number of neurons in the amygdala, a region of the brain that is involved in anxiety-related behaviors.
The COX inhibitor was also found to reduce the number and activity of the CB1 cannabinoid receptor, a cannabinoid receptor that is found on the surface of brain cells that are involved in the development of anxiety and depression.

People may experience a stressful or traumatic event that can cause anxiety in their day-to-day lives.
For some people, this anxiety can persist and develop into a disorder, such as posttraumatic stress disorder ( PTSD ) or acute stress disorder.
Millions of people worldwide suffer from these disorders, but there are few classes of drug treatments currently available.
Some preliminary studies in mice have suggested that anti-inflammatory drugs called cyclooxygenase-2 ( COX-2 ) inhibitors may relieve symptoms of anxiety.
COX-2 inhibitors target an enzyme called cyclooxygenase-2, which is produced in many tissues throughout the body and brain.
Some COX-2 inhibitors, such as Celebrex, are already widely used to treat arthritis.
Studies in people have also suggested these drugs given in combination with antidepressants might also help people with depression, while a recently developed COX-2 inhibitor, called LM-4131, stops the breakdown of chemicals produced in the brain that interact with the same brain receptors that marijuana does.
In animal studies, this new drug reduced anxious behaviors.
However, scientists didn’t know if the drug would also reduce anxious behaviors after animals were exposed to a traumatic or stressful experience.

Membrane proteins are the building blocks of membranes that connect cells to each other and to the outside world.
These proteins are essential for cell membranes to work properly, but they are difficult to work with due to their insolubility in aqueous solution and their poor stability in detergent micelles.
This has led researchers to develop a number of alternative methods for purifying membrane proteins, but these methods are expensive and time-consuming.
One such method is called the nanodisc, which uses a scaffold of proteins made of different lengths and different types of lipids to bind to membrane proteins.
However, this method can only work if the target membrane protein can be reconstituted into water-soluble particles that can be easily removed from the solution.
To achieve this, researchers need to find a way to reconstitute the membrane proteins into water soluble particles that are easy to remove from a solution.
One way to do this is to use a type of protein called a peptide, which is made up of a short chain of amino acids.
The peptide wraps around the membrane to form a structure called a bi-helical scaffold, which can then be used to repurpose the membrane protein into a new structure.
ref:
Surrounding every living cell is a biological membrane that is largely impermeable to water-soluble molecules.
This hydrophobic ( or “water-hating” ) barrier preserves the contents of the cell and also regulates how the cell interacts with its environment.
This latter function is critical and relies on a class of proteins that are embedded within the membrane and are also hydrophobic.
The hydrophobic nature of membrane proteins is however inconvenient for biochemical studies which usually take place in water-based solutions.
Therefore, membrane proteins are under-represented in biological research compared to the water-soluble ones, even though roughly one quarter of a cell’s proteins are membrane proteins.
Researchers have developed a few tricks to keep membrane proteins soluble after they have been extracted from the membrane.
An old but popular technique makes use of detergents, which are chemicals with opposing hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties ( hydrophilic literally means “water-loving” ).
However, even mild detergents can damage membrane proteins and will sometimes lead to experimental artifacts.
More recent tricks to stabilize membrane proteins without detergents have been described but remain laborious, costly or difficult to perform.

Transcription is the process by which DNA is copied into messenger RNA molecules ( or mRNAs ), which are then translated into proteins.
Errors in this process can lead to mutations in the genetic information encoded in the mRNA, which in turn can cause diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease and cancer.
However, it is not clear how many errors occur during the transcription of mRNA, or how they are caused by errors in the polymerase enzyme that makes the mRNA.
To find out more, Gout et al.
used a method called rolling-circle sequencing to identify RNA errors in bacteria and other prokaryotes.
The method uses a technique known as ‘rolling-circle amplification’ to amplify the amount of DNA that is being copied into mRNA molecules.
This allows the sequencing of the DNA to be done in real time, and the errors to be identified at a large scale.
The experiments were carried out in the bacteria Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis, Agrobacterium tumefaciensis, and Mesoplasma florum.
The results show that errors in transcription occur at a much higher rate than in other organisms.
The majority of errors occur at the 3′ end of mRNA molecules, which is where the
ref:
Most cells contain molecules of DNA that carry instructions to make the proteins cells need to perform different tasks.
When a cell requires a certain protein, the corresponding DNA sequence is first transcribed into molecules of ribonucleic acid ( RNA ) known as transcripts.
These sequences of RNA are then read by the cell and translated into the desired protein sequence.
Errors in copying DNA before a cell divides, can lead to genetic mutations that affect the ability of the cell to carry out certain roles, influencing the overall ‘fitness’ of the cell.
Similar to genetic mutations, errors that arise when forming RNA transcripts may also alter the tasks a cell performs.
However, it is difficult to find out what kinds of errors cells have in their transcripts and how often these mistakes occur.
This is because current methods for sequencing RNA are prone to technical inaccuracies that interfere with the ability to detect true transcript errors.
Now, Li and Lynch have adapted a method for high-throughput sequencing of RNA, which can accurately identify transcript errors in Escherichia coli and other species of bacteria.
The experiments showed that errors in RNA molecules occurred more frequently than genetic mutations in the same sequence of DNA.

After losing a hand, simple tasks such as tying your shoelaces become a daily challenge, resulting in serious implications for quality of life and employment.
How do different individuals adapt to such challenges, and what role does brain plasticity play in this adaptation?
By addressing these questions in individuals with congenital or acquired hand absence we hope to shed light on the scope for adaptive plasticity in the adult human brain, thereby providing important information for informing future neurorehabilitation strategies.
Previous studies have shown that the brain reorganises after the loss of a hand or arm.
However, these studies have largely focused on the brain’s response to sensory deprivation, and not on how the brain adapts to the absence of the hand or the residual arm.
This is because the brain can only reorganise in response to changes in the sensory environment, such as when a person is blind or deaf, but not when they have lost a hand.
It has been thought that brain reorganisation is restricted to the adult primary sensory cortex, the part of the brain responsible for vision and hearing.
This suggests that it is not possible for the brain to reorganise itself after a loss of one or both hands.
To address this issue, Jang et al.
studied the brain

The loss of a limb will have a profound impact on an individual’s daily life.
Nevertheless, individuals can employ a variety of behavioural strategies to adapt to the loss of, say, a hand.
Some become skilled at using the residual part of their arm, while others prefer to rely on their other hand.
Their brain, too, will undergo major changes.
Many studies have shown that the region of the brain that controlled a given limb can be “taken over” by another part of the body if that limb is lost.
This process has been previously considered to be harmful, as it has been linked to experiences of pain arising from the missing limb.
Now, Makin et al.
have explored the links between changes in the behaviour of individuals missing a hand and changes in their brains.
People who had been born without a hand or who had lost a hand in later life were asked to wear a device that recorded their movements as they went about their daily lives.
The data revealed that people who had been born without a hand made relatively more use of their residual limb, while those who had lost their hand made relatively more use of their remaining hand.
Moreover, these differences were reflected in patterns of brain activity.

Tetrapods are among the most studied animals in the animal kingdom because they have long bones that support their weight and allow them to move around on land.
The long bones are also important for the development of the limbs that support the weight of the animal and support the movement of the body.
However, it is not clear when these long bones started to produce blood cells.
The blood cells are produced in the bone marrow, which is found in the bones of mammals and many other animals, but not in fish or some aquatic tetrapods such as ray-finned fish.
Previous studies have suggested that the long bones of these animals may have started producing blood cells before the animals moved from the water to land.
Now, Kapp et al.
have used a technique called three-dimensional scanning electron microscopy to study the anatomy of long bones from the Permian period of the Earth’s history.
The experiments show that long bones in these animals were the first to produce red blood cells, and that they were also the first animals to have a centralised system of blood cells in their bone marrow.
This system is called haematopoiesis, and it is thought to have evolved in the early stages of tetrapod evolution
ref:
For many aquatic creatures, the red blood cells that rush through their bodies are created in organs such as the liver or the kidney.
In most land vertebrates however, blood-cell production occurs in the bone marrow.
There, the process is shielded from the ultraviolet light or starker temperature changes experienced out of the water.
It is possible that this difference evolved long before the first animal with a backbone crawled out of the aquatic environment and faced new, harsher conditions: yet very little fossil evidence exists to support this idea.
A definitive answer demands a close examination of fossils from the water-to-land transition including lobe-finned fish and early limbed vertebrates.
To support the production of red blood cells, their fin and limb bones would have needed an internal cavity that can house a specific niche that opens onto a complex network of blood vessels.
To investigate this question, Estefa et al.
harnessed the powerful x-ray beam produced by the European Synchrotron Radiation Facility and imaged the fin and limb bones from fossil lobe-finned fish and early limbed vertebrates.
The resulting three-dimensional structures revealed spongy long bones with closed internal cavities where the bone marrow cells were probably entrapped.

Alcohol use disorder is a major public health problem that affects millions of people around the world.
People with alcohol use disorder are more likely to experience anxiety, depression, and other negative emotions, and they are less likely to be able to cope with stress.
This is because the brain is highly sensitive to stress, and the stress response is blunted in people who have been exposed to excessive amounts of alcohol for long periods of time.
This blunting of the brain’s response to stress is a hallmark of alcohol use disorders, but it is not clear how the brain responds to stress.
To address this question, Lê et al.
have now used mice to study the brain activity of neurons in the stria terminalis, a brain region that is involved in regulating stress responses.
The mice were exposed to a compound called trimethylthiazoline ( TMT ), which is found in the feces of foxes.
This compound activates specific brain regions that are involved in stress, anxiety, and fear.
When TMT is added to the urine of the mice, the BNST starts to fire up, and these neurons are activated by the release of a molecule called Pdynorphin.

The connection between stress and alcohol use is highly complex.
On one hand, there is the idea of having a drink to ‘steady the nerves’.
On the other hand, in alcoholics, abnormal responses to stress often accompany heavy drinking.
In this case, it remains unknown whether stress cause excessive drinking, or vice versa.
Areas of the brain that normally help respond to stress work differently in long-term, heavy drinkers.
One example is a structure called the bed nucleus of the stria terminalis ( BNST ), which is over-active in anxiety disorders and is also associated with some of the symptoms of alcohol withdrawal.
The mechanism behind both problems is thought to be a specific ‘signaling system’ that is activated by a small molecule called dynorphin.
Previous research into the effects of dynorphin was performed either in the context of alcoholism or of anxiety disorders, but it was not known if there was a connection between the two.
Therefore, Hwa et al.
wanted to determine how prolonged alcohol use might affect responses to stress, and whether dynorphin signaling plays a role.

Heat shock is a phenomenon that occurs when the body is exposed to extreme temperatures.
In response to heat shock, cells release proteins called heat shock proteins that help to protect the body from damage.
These proteins are produced by a process called the heat shock response, which involves the activation of genes that encode proteins that act as heat shock chaperones.
When a cell experiences a high temperature, it activates a protein called HSP70, which activates the transcription of a gene that encodes the chaperone protein.
This protein is then translated into a molecule called mRNA, which is then exported to the nucleus, where it is processed by a machinery called the ribosome.
This process is controlled by a protein known as translation elongation factor eEF1A, which plays a key role in the production of many proteins.
However, it is not clear how this protein interacts with other proteins in the cell, and it was not clear whether it also plays a role in heat shock.
Now, Li et al.
have identified a new role for this protein in mammalian cells: it helps to regulate the entire process of heat shock activation, from transcription to translation.

ref:
Living cells must be able to withstand changes in the environment.
For example, if there is a sudden increase in temperature—which could damage proteins or other molecules—most cells can respond with the ‘heat shock response’.
In humans and other mammals, a single protein called heat shock factor 1 triggers the production of numerous heat shock proteins that protect the cell from the detrimental effects of high temperatures.
Most heat shock proteins protect cells by binding to, and stabilizing, other molecules in the cell; this prevents these molecules from being damaged or from aggregating and allows them to continue to function as normal.
A protein called eEF1A1 is involved in the final stages of protein production and also enhances the function of heat shock factor 1 during the heat shock response.
To make a protein, an enzyme called RNA polymerase transcribes DNA in the nucleus of the cell into a messenger RNA molecule that then exits the nucleus and binds to a ribosome.
This molecular machine then translates the messenger RNA sequence into a protein by joining together individual building blocks called amino acids in the correct order.
Like other elongation factors, eEF1A1 helps to select the amino acids that match the sequence of the messenger RNA template.

Flowers are important for plant reproductive success and for human sustenance.
Primordia that give rise to flowers are formed from stem cells that descend from the central stem cell pool.
These stem cells then divide to form new stem cells called primordia, which then divide again to form the new flower primordium.
The stem cells divide in a process called ‘transport amplifying’, which allows the stem cells to switch from the stem cell to the primordial stem cell.
This process is controlled by a hormone called auxin, which is produced by a group of proteins that bind to a protein called MONOPTEROS.
When auxin is present, it triggers a cascade of events that leads to the cell switching from stem cell descent to primordion founder cell descent.
In the absence of the auxin hormone, the cells do not switch to the correct stem cell descendent.
However, in the presence of the hormone, cells switch back to stem cells descendent, which are more likely to produce flowers.
This switch is triggered by the hormone auxin and by a transcription factor that is found on the surface of the cell.

Plants form new structures such as flowers or branches throughout their life as they develop and grow.
However, most plant cells are not able to produce a new flower or branch because the genes involved in these processes are usually switched off.
The genes are found in regions of chromatin—the structure in which DNA is packaged in plant cells—that are normally tightly packed.
This packing prevents other proteins called transcription factors from accessing the DNA and switching the genes on.
New flowers form from cells that contain high levels of a plant hormone called auxin.
In these cells, a protein called MONOPTEROS switches on genes involved in making flowers.
How the structure of the chromatin that surrounds these genes is altered so that they can be switched on is not clear.
Wu, Yamaguchi, Xiao et al.
studied this question in a plant known as Arabidopsis.
The experiments show that MONOPTEROS plays a crucial role in altering the structure of chromatin to allow flowers to form.
In the presence of high levels of auxin, MONOPTEROS recruits groups of proteins called SWI/SNF remodeling complexes to regions of chromatin that contain genes involved in flower formation.

Myelodysplastic syndromes are a group of genetic disorders that affect the ability of the immune system to recognize and destroy cancer cells.
Patients with these disorders often have mutations in the genes that control the production of certain types of white blood cells called myelocytes, which are responsible for fighting off cancer cells, such as leukemia and lymphoma.
However, these cells are not always able to divide properly, which can lead to the formation of new blood cells that are less able to fight off the cancer cells in the body.
In addition, some of these cells may not be able to produce enough of their own progenitor cells, which may contribute to the development of myeloma and other blood cancers.
Mutations in the chromosome 7 region of the human genome have been linked to these disorders, but it is not clear how these deletions affect myelocyte production or how they contribute to myeloid leukemia.
To investigate this question, Kere et al.
genetically engineered mice with a deletion of a section of chromosome 7 called 7q22, which is located on the long arm of the chromosome.
The mice were then genetically engineered to have a mutation in a gene called 5A3, which deletes a segment of 7q

Stem cells in the bone marrow are essential for creating new blood cells.
Myelodysplastic syndrome ( MDS ) is a common type of blood cancer in the elderly that occurs when blood cells fail to develop normally.
Depending on which types of blood cells are affected, individuals with MDS may bleed more easily, feel weak and tired, or be unable to effectively fight off infections.
Animals and plants store their genetic information in the form of chromosomes.
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes, with one copy inherited from the mother, and the other from the father.
The bone marrow cells of many people with MDS delete a section from one of their copies of chromosome 7.
As this section contains many different genes, it is difficult to fully understand which specific genes contribute to the development of MDS when one copy is lost.
Wong et al.
have now genetically engineered mice to lack a section of one of their copies of chromosome 7 that is often missing in patients with MDS.
Bone marrow cells from these mice exhibit many of the same abnormalities found in human MDS.
For example, most of the immature blood stem cells expand, but these stem cells do not correctly specialize into mature blood cells—in particular, not enough immune cells are produced.

The nervous system is a complex network of nerve cells that connects the brain and spinal cord to the body.
These nerve cells communicate with each other by sending electrical signals to each other and to other nerve cells in the body, which in turn send signals to other parts of the nervous system.
Sensory neurons in the brain are responsible for detecting and responding to touch, smell and sound, as well as for detecting heat, cold, chemical and mechanical stimuli in the environment.
These sensory neurons are also involved in the sense of touch and proprioception, which is the sensation of touch on the skin and the movement of the body’s muscles.
The somatosensory neurons are thought to play a key role in sensory perception, but it is not clear which types of neurons are involved in each of these sensory processes.
To investigate this, Abraira et al.
used a technique called transcriptional profiling to analyze the molecular signatures of individual neurons in a group of sensory neurons called dorsal root ganglion neurons ( DRG neurons ) in mice.
The experiments were carried out on mice that had been genetically engineered to produce a specific type of DRG neuron called the IB4+SNS-Cre/TdTomato+ cell type.

In the nervous system, a network of specialized neurons—known as the somatosensory system—carries information about sensations including touch, muscle position, temperature and pain.
Distinct sets of somatosensory neurons are thought to carry information about the different types of sensations.
In young animals, the precise switching on, or ‘expression’, of genes controls the formation of the network of neurons.
However, it is not known exactly which genes are expressed in what types of neurons, where, or when.
Here, Chiu et al.
used a technique called flow cytometry using different fluorescent markers to isolate a group of cells called Dorsal Root Ganglion ( DRG ) neurons in mice.
These neurons have long thread-like fibers that extend from the spinal cord to the skin, muscles and joints all over the body.
These fibers carry sensory information to the spinal cord, where it can be relayed to the brain and processed.
The experiments compared three distinct types of DRG neuron and found that they differed in their ability to send information to other cells.
Chiu et al.
analyzed the expression of all the genes in the three types of DRG neurons.

Cells are made up of two sets of chromosomes, each containing two copies of the same genetic material.
Each pair of chromosomes is made of two identical copies of DNA, which are then separated by a structure called a chiasma.
This structure is formed by a series of proteins called the synaptonemal complex, which assembles between the chromosomes to form a structure known as the ‘chiasma’.
During meiosis, each pair of homologous chromosomes undergoes at least one crossover ( crossover assurance ) exchange, which ensures that the two pairs of chromosomes are separated correctly.
However, in many species, the number of crossover exchanges is too small to ensure accurate chromosome segregation.
This is because the mechanism that ensures the correct number of crossovers is not well understood.
In the nematode worm Caenorhabditis elegans, for example, a family of proteins known as RING finger proteins is required to ensure that the correct amount of crossover exchange occurs.
These proteins are found in a region of the worm’s chromosome called the arm, which is located at the end of the chromosome.

Most human cells contain 23 pairs of chromosomes, giving 46 chromosomes in total.
When a cell divides, it typically copies all its chromosomes and distributes the copies equally between the two new cells, so that they also have 46 chromosomes.
Cells in the reproductive organs undergo a special division process called meiosis, which halves the number of chromosomes.
As a result, sperm and eggs have just 23 chromosomes, and so when they combine, the fertilized egg receives a complete set of 46 chromosomes.
A similar process happens in all species that use sexual reproduction.
As the chromosomes prepare to separate, they line up side by side in matching pairs.
During this period, DNA from one chromosome will swap with DNA from its partner.
These events are called “crossovers, ” and because such exchanges can happen at many locations along the chromosomes, no two sperm or eggs are the same.
Historic studies in fruit flies revealed that chromosomes do not mix their DNA at random.
After one crossover occurs, it is less likely that another will happen, and if there are two crossovers, the second one tends be far away from the first.
This suggests that there must be a signal that tells the chromosomes about the exchange.

Cells divide into two new cells at the end of each cell cycle.
This process is known as mitosis, and it involves a series of events called mitotic exit.
The first step in this process is the destruction of a group of proteins called the anaphase-promoting complex ( APC/C ) and the second step, mitotic dephosphorylation, which removes the phosphate groups from these proteins.
The second step is the removal of phosphatases, which are enzymes that add phosphate groups to the proteins that are active during mitosis.
However, it is not clear how these two processes are coordinated, and how they work together to ensure that the cell exits mitosis in a timely manner.
To find out, Sivakumar et al.
used a technique called high-resolution proteomics, which allows scientists to track the activity of proteins as they are destroyed.
The experiments show that the first stage of mitosis involves the proteolysis of a protein called cyclin B, followed by the phosphorylation of another protein called securin, and finally the removal from the cell membrane of a third protein called geminin.

New cells are made when a single cell duplicates its DNA and divides into two cells, distributing the DNA equally between them, in a process called mitosis.
The splitting of the two copies of DNA happens through a series of controlled events known as mitotic exit.
Previous research has suggested that mitotic exit relies on both the destruction of specific proteins and the removal of tags called phosphate groups from other proteins.
Phosphate groups modify how proteins behave and their removal can trigger changes in a protein’s activity.
Although protein destruction and phosphate group removal were known to be important to mitotic exit, it was not understood how they are coordinated in the cell to ensure the correct order of events.

have used a technique called mass spectrometry to monitor the level of thousands of proteins, and any tags attached to them, during mitotic exit in human cells grown in the laboratory.
The experiments revealed that the destruction of a single protein, known as cyclin B, plays a major role in triggering subsequent events.
The removal of cyclin B activates enzymes known as phosphatases, which remove phosphate groups from proteins.

Major depressive disorder ( MDD ) is a common illness that affects approximately 16% of the global population at some point in life.
Symptoms of MDD include lack of motivation, reduced ability to derive pleasure from natural rewards ( anhedonia ) and abnormalities of sleep and appetite.
It is thought to be caused by a combination of genetic, environmental, and biological factors, but it is not clear how these factors interact to cause MDD.
The brain region of the brain called the medial prefrontal cortex ( mPFC ) is known to be involved in depression and behavioral resilience.
However, it was not clear what role these cells play in the brain’s response to stress.
To address this question, it is important to understand the role of these cells in the development of depression and how they are affected by stress.
Previous studies have shown that depression is caused by mutations in a gene called Wfs1, which is found in a group of cells called the superficial layer 2/3 pyramidal cells.

Around 16% of people will experience an episode of major depression at some point in their lives, with symptoms including a loss of motivation, a reduced enjoyment of previously pleasurable activities, and disturbances in sleep and appetite.
Multiple genes and environmental factors have been implicated in depression, and one of the strongest risk factors for developing the disorder is exposure to stress.
Stress and depression affect many of the same brain regions, most notably the prefrontal cortex—an area that is involved in decision making, problem solving and regulating emotions.

therefore reasoned that a good way of obtaining insights into the relationship between stress and depression would be to study prefrontal cortex cells that express genes that have been linked to depression.

identified a specific population of cells in the prefrontal cortex that express Wfs1.

Proteins are the building blocks of all living cells, and they are essential for life as we know it.
A protein is made up of a chain of amino acids, which are arranged in a three-dimensional ( 3D ) structure.
The structure of a protein is determined by a series of chemical modifications that are made to the amino acids at specific sites on the protein.
These modifications are known as post-translational modifications ( or PTMs ) and they affect the structure of the protein in a number of different ways.
For example, PTMs can change the shape of specific amino acids in the protein, or they can change how the protein interacts with other proteins in the cell.
However, the effects of PTMs are not always localized to the specific site where they are made.
Instead, they can also affect the whole protein, which can affect how it behaves in different ways depending on the environment.
The chaperone protein Hsp90 is an important part of the cell’s protein machinery, and it is involved in the maturation of 20% of the entire proteome.

Proteins play a wide variety of roles in the cell and interact with many other molecules.
The behavior of proteins depends on their structure; yet, proteins are often flexible and will change shape, much like a tree in the wind.
Nevertheless, for some of the activities that it performs, a protein must adopt one specific shape.
Therefore, the likelihood that the protein will take on this specific shape directly determines how efficiently that protein can perform a specific job.
The shape of a protein can be regulated by changes at several levels; these could include modifying one of the amino acid building blocks that make up that protein, binding to another protein, or by placing the protein in a part of the cell that is crowded with other large molecules.
Schmid and Hugel wanted to understand how these three different types of regulation affect the structure of a protein and how they relate to its activities.
The protein Hsp90 was used as a test case.

Cells are made up of many different types of cells, each with a unique set of specialized structures that enable them to perform specific roles.
For example, neurons in the brain are able to form specialized structures called ‘neurons’, while leukocytes can divide into two distinct groups of cells.
The front of a cell is called the front, and the back of the cell is known as the back.
When a cell divides into two, the front of each of the two new cells becomes the ‘back’ of the new cell, while the back becomes the front.
This process is thought to be triggered by fluctuations in the concentration of a protein called Cdc42, which is responsible for the formation of a region of the front called the cortex.
However, it is not clear how this process works in yeast cells, which do not have a natural polarity system.
Instead, yeast cells use a synthetic form of polarity that is made from a fusion of two different proteins.

The cells of fungi and other eukaryotic organisms are generally asymmetric in shape and structure.
This is possible because newly-formed cells can establish front and back ends that influence how each cell grows and develops.
A protein called Cdc42 – which can cycle between an active and inactive form – establishes this cell “polarity”.
The front of the cell is specified by the accumulation of active Cdc42 proteins in a small area of the membrane that surrounds the cell.
However, it is not clear why the accumulation of active Cdc42 only happens in one place.

This yeast reproduces through the budding of new daughter cells out of one end of the mother cell.
To achieve this, the mother establishes a single front end through the accumulation of active Cdc42.
This marks the site where the daughter cell will emerge.
Previous research has shown that during the early stages of cell polarization, Cdc42 clusters start to form at several places on the inner surface of the membrane.
Only a single cluster emerges as the winner, while the others lose and shrink away.

When DNA is damaged, it can be repaired using a process called homologous recombination, which involves the exchange of base pairs between two DNA molecules.
This process is called strand exchange, and it is carried out by a protein called RecA.

The DNA molecules in cells are continuously bombarded with radiation, chemicals and other agents, and it is important for cells to repair the damage caused by these before the process of cell division begins.
Most DNA molecules consist of two single strands of DNA that are held together by hydrogen bonds in the familiar double-helix structure.
Of the various types of damage that DNA molecules are prone to, double-strand breaks are among the most dangerous because they can lead to cancer if they are not repaired.
DNA molecules use four bases—adenine, cytosine, guanine, and thymine—to store genetic information.
In single-stranded DNA these bases are attached to a backbone made of alternating sugar and phosphate groups.
A crucial feature of double-stranded DNA is that the sequences of bases in the two strands are complementary to each other—adenine is always paired with thymine, and cytosine is always paired with guanine.
However, the hydrogen bonds that hold the pairs of bases together are quite weak, which means that the two strands of the double helix can be pulled apart quite easily.
The ease with which these bonds can be formed and broken is crucial for many genetic processes.

Pneumoviruses are a group of viruses that infect the lungs and other organs of humans and other mammals, and are a major cause of acute respiratory tract infections in children, elderly and immunocompromised populations worldwide.
These viruses contain a large number of proteins that are essential for their ability to replicate in the body.
The viral genome contains the genetic information needed to make all the proteins needed for the virus to infect the host.
The virus uses a process called RNA polymerase to make copies of its genetic material, which is then used as a template for the replication of the virus.
This process is carried out by a protein called M2-1, which encodes a zinc-binding protein that is essential for the viral replication process.
However, it is not clear how this protein is able to bind to the DNA and regulate the activity of the viral genome.

used a technique called X-ray crystallography to study the structure of a protein from a human metapneumovirus called HMPV.

To produce a protein from a gene, the gene must first be transcribed to make a molecule of RNA.

When a virus replicates, however, it needs to transcribe all the genes in its genome, so it relies on antiterminator proteins to make the enzyme building the RNA ignore the termination signal.
Therefore, medicines that stop antiterminators working could stop viral infections spreading.
Human metapneumovirus ( HMPV ) can cause severe respiratory infections in children, the elderly and people with weakened immune systems.

Lysosomes are organelles in the cell membrane that are responsible for the breakdown and recycling of cellular machinery.
Dysfunctional lysosome systems can lead to diseases such as cystic fibrosis, myotonia, epilepsy, hyperekplexia or deafness.
Dysfunction of lyssomes can also lead to neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’ disease, where the body cannot properly recycle the waste products of the cell.
Chloride is the most abundant, soluble anion in the body, and it helps to balance the levels of ions in the cells.
However, it is difficult to measure chloride levels in living cells because they are tightly regulated by channels that allow chloride ions to flow into and out of cells.
The levels of chloride in cells are controlled by channels called chloride channels, which open and close in response to changes in the pH of the cells, which in turn affects the activity of specific enzymes that break down waste products.
The amount of chloride inside the cell is also tightly regulated, but it is not clear how much chloride is needed to balance chloride levels within the cell and how this affects how the cells work.

In cells, worn out proteins and other unnecessary materials are sent to small compartments called lysosomes to be broken down and recycled.
Lysosomes contain many different proteins including some that break down waste material into recyclable fragments and others that transport the fragments out of the lysosome.
If any of these proteins do not work, waste products build up and cause disease.
There are around 70 such lysosomal storage diseases, each arising from a different lysosomal protein not working correctly.

– including some of the researchers who developed Clensor – have used this nanodevice to examine chloride ion levels in the lysosomes of the roundworm Caenorhabditis elegans.

When we are in a social situation, we often need to shift our attention from one place to the next.
To do this, we rely on cues provided by the other person, such as the direction of their gaze or the position of their head.
For example, if a person is looking at an object in front of him/her, he/she will follow his/her gaze towards the object of interest, while he/her head will be facing away from the object.
This is called ‘geometric gaze following’, and it is thought to be similar to gaze following in humans.
However, it is not clear whether this is the case in monkeys, or if it is more likely to be the case that monkeys follow the gaze of their peers.
To find out, Liuzza et al.
used functional magnetic resonance imaging ( fMRI ) to study the brain activity of rhesus macaque monkeys.
The experiments show that monkeys can follow their peers’ gaze to an object by following the head gaze of the person they are looking at.

Gaze following—working out where someone else is looking, and then switching your attention to that position—is an important part of social behavior and learning.
Additionally, it is thought to be an important step towards recognizing that others have a mind of their own.
Humans mostly use eye position to work out the ‘gaze direction’ of someone else, whereas non-human primates rely instead on the orientation of the face.
However, the neural circuits that control gaze following are thought to be similar in both.
Gaze following is a complex process that requires the brain to process a lot of different information.
A face must be recognized, and its orientation worked out.
A series of complex geometrical calculations must then be performed to work out the direction of the gaze, and how this relates to the position of the observer.
Finally, the object of interest needs to be recognized and the attention of the observer focused on it.
In the monkey brain, there are six interconnected areas called face patch regions that respond when a monkey is shown a face.
However, researchers do not understand how monkeys translate the information about face orientation gathered by these regions into information about where to look during gaze following.

In humans and other mammals, one eye of the eye that is closed during early life is affected by a condition known as amblyopia.
This condition causes the brain to lose the ability to process information from the other eye, and the brain’s ability to respond to visual stimuli is impaired.
If this condition persists for many years, it can lead to vision loss in adulthood.
However, it is possible to improve the recovery of vision in adults by stimulating the visual cortex of the deprived eye.
This is possible because the brain is able to generate more electrical activity during movement than when the eyes are closed, and it is thought that the brain can use this extra energy to compensate for the loss of vision from the closed eye.

Amblyopia, otherwise known as ‘lazy eye’, is a condition in which vision fails to develop normally during childhood, not due to problems with the eye itself but due to problems with the transmission of information from the eye to the brain.
It occurs when disorders such as squint—in which the eyes point in different directions—cause the brain to continually ignore input from one eye, with the result that vision in that eye never fully develops.
If detected in infancy, amblyopia can be treated by surgery, although such interventions must be performed early because they are much less effective when used on adults.

The leaf-cutting ants of the genus Acromyrmex live in a symbiotic relationship with a fungus called Leucocoprinus gongylophorus, which is found in tropical rainforests in South America.
The fungus feeds on decaying leaves, which the ants then use to make new leaves for the fungus to grow on in their gardens.
However, most of the leaves that the ants harvest are heavily protected against herbivory by recalcitrant cell walls and toxic substances ( Chen, 2008 ), which means that the fungus is unable to digest them.
This leaves the ants with a lot of waste, which they use to feed on the fungus.
The ants use their own fecal fluid to break down the leaves, and the fungus then uses this waste as a growth substrate to grow new leaves.
This process is carried out by enzymes that are made from the ants’ own feces, but it is not clear how the fungus and the ants work together.

Colonies of tropical leaf-cutting ants live in underground nests where a fungus grows that feeds them.
The ants, in turn, provide the fungus with the freshly-cut leaf fragments it needs for nutrition.
The relationship between the ants and the fungus, in which they live close together and help one another survive, is known as symbiosis.
It is an ancient, extremely well integrated relationship, in which neither species can survive without the other.
However, the details of how the ants and the fungus work together to break down the leaf fragments so they can be used for nutrition are not well understood.
When the ants eat the fungus, they do not digest its enzymes ( the proteins that accelerate chemical reactions in a cell ).
Instead, the fungal enzymes travel through the ants’ gut and into their fecal liquid, which gets deposited on the fresh-cut leaves when the ants collect them.
The ants then make temporary pellets out of the new leaf fragments before providing them to the fungus.

Antibiotics are commonly used in the treatment of bacterial infections, and, in effectively combating such diseases, have substantially increased human life expectancy.
However, they can also interfere with the body’s immune system, leading to adverse side-effects, including nausea, skin reactions, and gastrointestinal and neurological disorders.
Antibiotics can also alter the metabolism of the bacteria that cause the infections, which can lead to the development of antibiotic resistance.
This is a major threat to global health care as the antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections become less effective.
To counter this, new antibiotics are being developed and brought to the clinic.
One of these drugs, bedaquiline, is an antibiotic that works by inhibiting the activity of a protein called ATP synthase, which is essential for bacteria to multiply.
It has been shown that it can also affect the immune system by changing the genes of immune cells called macrophages, which are specialized cells that help the body to fight off infections.

The discovery of antibiotic drugs, which treat diseases caused by bacteria, has been a hugely valuable advance in modern medicine.
They work by targeting specific cellular processes in bacteria, ultimately stopping them from multiplying or killing them outright.
Antibiotics sometimes also affect their human hosts and can cause side-effects, such as gut problems or skin reactions.
Recent evidence suggests that antibiotics also have an impact on the human immune system.
This may happen either indirectly, by affecting ‘friendly’ bacteria normally present in the body, or through direct effects on immune cells.
In turn, this could change the effectiveness of drug treatments.
For example, if an antibiotic weakens immune cells, the body could have difficulty fighting off the existing infection – or become more vulnerable to new ones.
However, even though new drugs are being introduced to combat the worldwide rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, their effects on immunity are still not well understood.

The lung is made up of a layer of cells called the epithelium, which is surrounded by the mesenchymal layer, which consists of the cells that make up the lining of the lungs.
The epithelial layer of the lung is composed of many different types of cells, but it is not clear how these cells coordinate with each other to make the lung grow and develop into the lung that we see today.

This process is called branching morphogenesis, and it involves many different ways in which the cells in the lung can form branches.

Air enters our lungs through a system of airways that spread outwards from the windpipe like the branches of a tree.
Before we are born, each branch is shaped by the organization and movement of cells that form the walls of the airways, called epithelial cells.
This process requires the cells to communicate and coordinate with each other by receiving and/or sending chemical signals.
One important system that epithelial cells use to communicate is called the Hippo pathway, which uses a molecule called YAP to execute received messages.

Bacteria are a group of bacteria that can cause serious diseases, such as food poisoning and diarrhea, because they are able to use a type of protein called an injectisome to inject harmful molecules into their host cells.
The injectisomes are made up of three parts: a needle-like structure that protrudes from the bacterial surface, a membrane that spans the two bacterial membranes and the periplasm, and a cytoplasmic part that connects the two membranes to form a structure called the outer membrane.
The outer membrane is made of a barrel-shaped structure, while the inner membrane contains a ring-shaped protein called secretin, which is folded into a ring at the outermost part of the cell membrane.
Inside the bacterial cell, the outer and inner membranes are separated by a membrane called the innermost membrane, which contains the cell’s genetic material.
The inner membrane of the bacterial cells contains a protein called YscC, which acts as a connector between the inner and outer membranes.

Humans and other animals can use the five senses—touch, taste, sight, smell, and hearing—to interpret the world around them.
Single-celled organisms, however, must rely on molecular cues to understand their immediate surroundings.
In particular, bacteria gather information about external conditions, including potential hosts nearby, by secreting protein sensors that can relay messages back to the cell.
Bacteria export these sensors via secretion systems that enable the organism both to receive information about the environment and to invade a host cell.
A total of seven separate secretion systems, known as types I–VII, have been identified.
These different secretion systems handle distinct cargoes, allowing the bacterial cell to respond to a range of feedback from the external milieu.
The type III secretion system, also known as the ‘injectisome’, is found in bacterial species that are enclosed by two membranes separated by a periplasmic space.
The injectisome comprises different components that combine to form the basal body, which spans the inner and outer membranes, and a projection from the basal body, called the hollow needle, that mediates the export of cargo from a bacterium to its host or the local environment.

Arabidopsis thaliana is a plant that is used as a model organism to study plant growth, development and reproduction.

Over a billion people worldwide are infected, often for life, by a parasite known as Toxoplasma gondii.
Most of the time, the parasites remain in a dormant state and cause no symptoms.
However, the parasites can reactivate, which can be fatal for people with weaker immune systems.
Currently no drugs can kill the dormant stages of T. gondii.
One of the possible reasons T. gondii is so widespread is because it easily passes from host to host, and resists both detection and treatment.
Like many parasites, T. gondii infects multiple hosts during its life cycle: it reproduces sexually in its ‘definitive' host, the cat, and reproduces asexually in its ‘intermediate’ hosts, which include virtually all warm-blooded animals.
Unlike most parasites, all hosts can transmit T. gondii to other hosts, making it a very flexible parasite.
However, its closest living relative, Hammondia hammondi, can only be transmitted from a definitive host to an intermediate host, or vice versa.
The reason for this difference remains unclear, but comparisons between these species can hopefully help uncover how T. gondii came to be such a promiscuous parasite.

How does the brain rid itself of waste products?
Other organs in the body achieve this via a system called the lymphatic system.
A network of lymphatic vessels extends throughout the body in a pattern similar to that of blood vessels.
Waste products from cells, plus bacteria, viruses and excess fluids drain out of the body’s tissues into lymphatic vessels, which transfer them to the bloodstream.
Blood vessels then carry the waste products to the kidneys, which filter them out for excretion.
Lymphatic vessels are also a highway for circulation of white blood cells, which fight infections, and are therefore an important part of the immune system.
Unlike other organs, the brain does not contain lymphatic vessels.

Vision is a highly complex, active process.
As we observe and interact with the world around us, we constantly use eye movements to capture the visual information we need.
In fact, our eyes continue to make tiny, unconscious movements even when we try to fix our gaze on something.
There are two main types of tiny eye movements.
The first kind, so called microsaccades, are fast, microscopic flicks that happen every second or half-second.
The other kind, termed drift, is a slower, gradual motion that takes place between microsaccades, or at any time when other eye movements are not happening.

Many animals make use of chemical signals to communicate with other members of their species.
Such chemical signals, called pheromones, often allow males and females of the same species to recognize each other before mating.
Since the discovery of the very first pheromone in the silkworm moth Bombyx mori at the end of the 1950s, moths have been a model for pheromone research.
The sex pheromone communication system in these insects has thus been well described: females emit a mixture of volatile chemicals, which can be detected by the antennae of males up to several hundred meters away.
This detection is achieved through neurons with specialized proteins known as pheromone receptors that bind to the chemical signals produced by the females.

The evolution of pheromone signals and their corresponding receptors can therefore lead to the rise of new reproductive barriers between populations, and eventually to the emergence of new species.

Cell membranes are made up of two layers of molecules called lipid bilayers.
The outer leaflet of the membrane is made of proteins called phosphatidylcholine and sphingomyelin, while the inner leaflet contains a mixture of other molecules called phospholipids.
This asymmetry in the distribution of these molecules is essential for the cell to work properly.
In eukaryotes, for example, this asymmetry is established by a group of proteins known as ATP-dependent lipid flippases, which transport the phosphatids between the two leaflets.
However, there are also ATP-independent proteins that help to regulate the movement of the phosphates from one leaflet to the other.

Cell membranes are made of two layers of molecules called phospholipids.
The types of phospholipid molecules in the outer layer are often different from those in the inner layer.
This asymmetry is an important feature of most membranes, but cells also have proteins called ‘scramblases’ that can move ( or scramble ) the phospholipids between the two layers.
This scrambling process often marks a cell for destruction but also plays a key role in many activities throughout the body including cell–cell fusion, blood clotting, autoimmune diseases and inflammation.
Previous research revealed that a membrane protein called ANO6 is needed for some kinds of phospholipid scrambling.
Other proteins that are most closely related to ANO6 are not phospholipid scramblases; instead they are channel proteins that allow ions to pass across cell membranes.
ANO6 can also allow ions to flow across membranes, which raised the question: is ANO6 actually a scramblase itself, or does it control other proteins with scramblase activity?
Yu, Whitlock et al.
addressed this question by engineering human cells grown in the laboratory to produce the ANO6 protein, and found that these cells had high levels of phospholipid scrambling.

Branching morphogenesis is an important part of the development of many organs, including the kidneys, lungs and mammary glands.
This process begins with the formation of a bud-like structure known as the ureteric bud, which then grows and divides to form a network of specialized cells known as nephrons.
Nephrons then connect to each other to form the collection system of the adult kidney, which helps to regulate the flow of urine and other bodily fluids.

This suggests that branching occurs in a stochastic manner in many organs.

During development and before birth, many organs develop from branched tubes.
Whether forming the airways of the lungs, the collecting ducts of the kidneys or the milk ducts of the breast, there are many similarities between these structures.
Given their shared tree-like structures, one possibility is that these tissues all form through the same general process.
A key challenge is understanding why branched networks develop and pattern in such a way as to assume their functional roles in the adult organ.
A unifying theory, which proposes that certain tips stop growing in a random manner, has been proposed to solve this problem.
In this theory, the branched mammary gland structures stop growing when the tips of the structure impinge on neighbouring branches.

The cellular Potts model ( CPM ) is a powerful in silico method for simulating biological processes at tissue scale.
However, the CPM is not widely used because it requires a specialised computer program to run it.
Instead, CPMs are often implemented in specialised frameworks, which require the programmer to master the C++ programming language before they can run the model.
This makes it difficult for researchers to share CPM simulations with other researchers, and it also makes it harder for scientists to understand how the model works.

For example, cellular Potts models ( or CPMs for short ) are regularly used to simulate how cells move, self-organise to form tissues and respond to their surroundings.
Computational biologists use a range of specialist skills and software to create these models.

Parkinson's disease is the second most common neurodegenerative illness in the world.
The disease is caused by the loss of dopamine neurons in the brainstem, which are responsible for the release of the neurotransmitter dopamine.
However, Parkinson’s disease also affects other parts of the brain, including the midbrain, the basal ganglia, and the cortex.

The inner surface of the vertebrate eye is lined with a multilayered structure known as the retina.
The bottom layer of the retina is composed of rods and cones—neurons that are directly sensitive to light—and is called the photoreceptor layer.
Rods function primarily in dim light and provide black-and-white vision, while cones support daytime vision and are responsible for colour perception.
Unlike the upper layers of the retina, the photoreceptor layer does not contain blood vessels: oxygen and nutrients are instead provided by a structure just underneath the retina called the choroid.
The eye relies on the rods and cones converting light into electrical signals, and the photoreceptor layer must remain free of blood vessels for this process to work properly.
If blood vessels extend into the photoreceptor layer from rest of the retina ( which is above it ) or the choroid ( below ), they can disrupt the retina and give rise to a condition called age-related macular degeneration, which is a leading cause of irreversible blindness in adults.
